items.q: What is a brand in marketing?
items.correct: A combination of name, design, symbols and associations that distinguishes a product and creates customer perception and loyalty — more than just a logo.
items.misconception: A brand is just the logo on a product.
items.random_wrong: A brand is the company that makes a product.
items.why: Branding is the sum of perceptions, not just visual identity. Coca-Cola the brand is much more than the script logo — it is associations, taste expectations, advertising history.

items.correct: The level of output where total revenue equals total costs — neither profit nor loss. It is NOT the level where profit is maximised; profit grows beyond break-even.
items.misconception: Break-even is where the business makes the most profit.
items.random_wrong: Break-even is where the business owes no debt.
items.why: Break-even is a no-profit-no-loss point, useful for risk analysis. Students conflate it with profit maximisation. Going above break-even is where profit starts; below break-even means a loss.

items.q: Why does a company's share price change?
items.correct: Share prices reflect investors' expectations about future profits — driven by company performance, sector trends, economic conditions, and market sentiment.
items.misconception: Share prices go up when a company makes more money this year.
items.random_wrong: Share prices are set by the government each morning.
items.why: Markets are forward-looking. A company can report record profits and see shares fall if results were worse than expected. Students miss the expectations-driven nature of pricing.

items.q: Starting cheap to grab market share is…
items.why: Correct answer: Penetration. “Skimming” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Apple charging high for new iPhones is…
items.why: Correct answer: Skimming. “Cost-plus” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
